summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Runny nose and stuffy nose.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports a runny nose and stuffy nose that started around supper time last night. He also reports pulling on his ears and experiencing some irritability. He has been using saline mist and aveno shampoo on his cradle cap. He has taken Tylenol this morning to reduce fever. He reports varying appetite, eating about seven ounces of formula this morning and barely any cereal. His older sister has been sick, and he had a playdate with her. He has been giving the baby Vicks on his feet. He is currently suspecting a viral infection.

Findings
The patient's rectal temperature was 100.3°F. He has cradle cap, which is worsening. Examination reveals clear mucous. He is breathing normally.

Assessment
Clinical presentation is most consistent with a viral infection, likely a sinus infection. The doctor suspects a sinus infection.

Plan
The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine. Follow up in one week if symptoms persist. If a barky cough develops, administer half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Runny nose and stuffy nose.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports a runny nose and stuffy nose that started around supper time last night. He also reports pulling on his ears and experiencing some irritability. He has been using saline mist and aveno shampoo on his cradle cap. He has taken Tylenol this morning to reduce fever. He reports varying appetite, eating about seven ounces of formula this morning and barely any cereal. His older sister has been sick, and he had a playdate with her. He has been giving the baby Vicks on his feet. He is currently suspecting a viral infection.
Findings
The patient's rectal temperature was 100.3°F. He has cradle cap, which is worsening. Examination reveals clear mucous. He is breathing normally.
Assessment
Clinical presentation is most consistent with a viral infection, likely a sinus infection. The doctor suspects a sinus infection.
Plan
The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine. Follow up in one week if symptoms persist. If a barky cough develops, administer half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.

section_miscategorizations.text: He has been using saline mist and aveno shampoo on his cradle cap.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Use of saline mist and Aveeno shampoo represents ongoing treatment/management rather than symptoms or chronology; it fits better as part of treatment plan or current regimen, though its presence in HPI is not seriously misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: He has taken Tylenol this morning to reduce fever.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a medication already taken for symptom control (current treatment). It is more appropriately documented as part of management/medication use, but inclusion in HPI as context is not highly misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: He is currently suspecting a viral infection.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an etiologic impression (even if from the patient) rather than a symptom description. It aligns more with diagnostic thinking, but its placement in HPI does not substantially distort clinical meaning.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is not an appropriate or realistic medical plan and appears to be a misstatement of clinical intent rather than a true treatment order. As written, it could dangerously mislead a reader into thinking that using another child's medication is an endorsed plan; it should not appear as a formal plan item.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Similar to the prior statement, this reads as an instruction to administer another child's medication, which is clinically inappropriate as a plan. Its presence in the Plan section could mislead a reader into believing this is a sanctioned treatment step.

section_miscategorizations.text: If a barky cough develops, administer half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This conditional instruction to use another child's medicine is framed as anticipatory guidance/plan. Because it endorses an unsafe and nonstandard action, its inclusion as a formal plan item is misleading and inappropriate for the Plan section.
